reason: The statement applies because the text describes the Israelites led by Saul coming up from Egypt and encountering the Amalekites in battle. Saul was given a command by the LORD to utterly destroy the Amalekites for what they did to Israel when they came up from Egypt. The text also mentions Saul sparing Agag, the king of the Amalekites, and the best of the sheep and oxen, going against the LORD's command. This narrative aligns with Hebrew returnees entering the Hebrew land from its east, as they were returning from Egypt to the land of Israel, encountering the Amalekites along the way.
